posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
From Jancis in Sat FT on 2014 burg (first of two); fine enough articlem but nothing as surprising as this claim:

“Even Frédéric Engerer, Pinault’s president of Château Latour, confesses he can no longer afford to drink burgundies above village level in a restaurant.”

REALLY? Maybe in the restaurants he frequents? Still…

Well, he doesn’t own it, he works for François Pinault right? I imagine he’s paid pretty well but a lot of the miners in Africa probably can’t afford to buy the diamonds they dig up once they’re cut and set.

Can’t feel too bad for him though - what’s Latour going for these days?

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

From Jancis in Sat FT on 2014 burg (first of two); fine enough articlem but nothing as surprising as this claim:

“Even Frédéric Engerer, Pinault’s president of Château Latour, confesses he can no longer afford to drink burgundies above village level in a restaurant.”

REALLY? Maybe in the restaurants he frequents? Still…

What do you question about this statement? Premier Cru level or above is probably +$300/bottle. who knows what Engerer is paid? Maybe more at some places (French Laundry, Per Se, RN74). My recollection of wine prices in France is hazier–at Ma Cuisine in Beaune one might find the occasional 1er Cru at closer to 100 Euros, but…

posts.name: Glenn L e v i n e
posts.cooked: I’m with Greg, I can’t afford Latour or Les Forts in any restaurant! Welcome to my world Frédéric.
posts.display_username: Glenn L e v i n e

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
No one’s talking about Latour or Les Fort but 1er Cru burg. From Pearl and Ash (NYC! restaurant), here is a bunch of PYMC (whom JR was plumping in the article):
saint-aubin 1er cru, “les créots” 2013 125
saint-aubin 1er cru, “les champlots” 2013 125
saint-aubin 1er cru, “chatenière” 2012 155
saint-aubin 1er cru, “chatenière” 2010 155

saint-aubin 1er cru, “en remilly” 2010 155

chassagne-montrachet 1er cru, “champs gain” 2012 200
chassagne-montrachet 1er cru, “les chenevottes” 2013 200
chassagne-montrachet 1er cru, “les chenevottes” 2012 200
chassagne-montrachet 1er cru, “les baudines” 2011 185

And here are some reds:
dominique lafon, beaune 1er cru, “les epenots” 2011 135
yann durieux, côtes de nuits village, “les 1er ponts” 2011 135
henri germain, meursault, “les mouches” 2012 145
grivot, nuits-st. george 1er cru, “les prulièrs” 2008 155
bachelet, gevrey-chambertin, “vieilles vignes” 2004 160
daniel rion, vosne-romanée 1er cru, “les chaumes” 1999 170
genot-boulanger, corton 2011 180
comtes lafon, volnay 2011 180
marquis d’angerville, volnay 1er cru, “frémiets” 2011 180

I can and do buy in this range (at this restaurant in fact) and defies belief that the directing manager of Latour could not afford to.(Plus for him its tax deductible.)

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Maybe he has a horrific gambling problem and can’t really afford anything else.

Seriously, that comment doesn’t make sense.
I love high-end Burgundy. I cannot remember the last time I ordered one in a restaurant.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked: Well, yes, I said, maybe he is eating at the wrong restaurants, but plenty of spots in Paris along the lines of Pearl and Ash (or Racines or Tribeca Grill for that matter)–not to mention the old school places, the European equivalent to Bern’s, etc.
Maybe “what my expense account allows” got lost in translation…

posts.name: Peter Shurman
posts.cooked: damn, another first world problem hits the limelight … wuwt
On top of the wines being prohibitively expensive, I also seldom order them in restaurants because they are too young.

posts.name: maureen nelson
posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

(Plus for him its tax deductible.)

Wow, French tax law must be really generous to let someone deduct the cost of a bottle of wine consumed with dinner from taxable income!

posts.name: M McCombs
posts.cooked:
jcoley3:

On top of the wines being prohibitively expensive, I also seldom order them in restaurants because they are too young.

Amen on this. Proportionally I drink more more zin and U.S. Pinot than anything else when dining out. A popular steak place in St Louis has already burned through most of their 2010 barolos. I split a case of 2010 Cavallotto with my dad at about $53 per. It is at the very back of my storage closet, not to be touched for years.
They had it for $140 and it was sold out by Labor Day.
Don’t recall seeing anything other than first growth bdx with more than 10 years on them. The Burgundy pricing is downright frightening. I feel I should check my wallet after looking at the wine list.
More power to them I guess. Maybe we should thank them?
The Rafanelli zin is expensive compared to other places but a downright bargain compared to other stuff.

On top of the wines being prohibitively expensive, I also seldom order them in restaurants because they are too young.

posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

Well, yes, I said, maybe he is eating at the wrong restaurants, but plenty of spots in Paris along the lines of Pearl and Ash (or Racines or Tribeca Grill for that matter)–not to mention the old school places, the European equivalent to Bern’s, etc.

If you have some destinations in Paris with good Premier Cru Burg pricing, let me know…

Lots of ITB people that don’t want to spend much on wine.
Lots of ITB people that don’t like or care about wine.

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked: Maybe M. Engerer is familiar with French retail prices for Burgundies of that level and just has a hard time paying the restaurant premiums. Not every one spends like there’s no tomorrow.

posts.name: Michae1 P0wers
posts.cooked: I think the statement is dubious as well, and perhaps an example of a Bordelais taking a shot at Burgundy. Alternatively, perhaps he drinks only top producers, which would change the math by quite a bit.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Michael Powers:

I think the statement is dubious as well, and perhaps an example of a Bordelais taking a shot at Burgundy. Alternatively, perhaps he drinks only top producers, which would change the math by quite a bit.

True, hadn’t considered that. If he only drinks DRC, Comte Liger Belair, and Roumier then I can understand the statement.

But as written it’s nonsense.

Michael Powers:

I think the statement is dubious as well, and perhaps an example of a Bordelais taking a shot at Burgundy. Alternatively, perhaps he drinks only top producers, which would change the math by quite a bit.

Wouldn’t be the best way to endear himself to his boss, who owns the rather extravagantly-priced Domaine d’Eugenie in Vosne, too.

posts.name: Greg D
posts.cooked:
It’s probably more the case that he can’t stomach paying the prices for the marked up higher priced wines. He really CAN afford them, but doesn’t want to pay the price.

That’s the case for me. Sure… I can AFFORD to pay $200-$250 at a restaurant for a $100 retail wine, but I choose not to. Too damned expensive. I have other things I’d rather spend that money on.

Greg D:

It’s probably more the case that he can’t stomach paying the prices for the marked up higher priced wines. He really CAN afford them, but doesn’t want to pay the price.

That’s the case for me. Sure… I can AFFORD to pay $200-$250 at a restaurant for a $100 retail wine, but I choose not to. Too damned expensive. I have other things I’d rather spend that money on.

Exactly. Assuming we’re talking about the vast majority of wine lists around the world, where prices are high and wines are young, I’d rather get a village Burgundy, where I feel I’m wasting less of a wine’s eventual upside, and take the rest of the money not spent on a 1er Cru in a restaurant, spend it on one at retail and drink that after cellaring it properly and serving it the way I would like.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: While the broader discussion this quotation raises is certainly interesting, I would caution against taking Monsieur Engerer’s statement too literally.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: It’s worth considering, just as a remote possibility, that the remark was a quip, nothing more. A small bit of hyperbole in regards to current pricing. i.e. not to be taken literally…

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Well, maybe he´s also drinking Château Pibran or Fleur-Milon instead of Château Latour in any restaurant …

Seriously: the prices in most restaurants (worldwide) are simply too high … as good as the food is in many cases - it is simply no fun to pay 300/400+ bucks for a) often far too young) bottle that I have in my own cellar for 70,- …

There are some wealthy people who don´t care at all, but I´m always looking for the (occosional) value on most wine cards … or I bring my own whenever possible …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked: How familiar are you with French salaries? Cost of living in France? And the cultural context of spending €100 on a bottle of wine in a restaurant for french people? Are you aware of how much Latour + Burgundy is exported versus how much is consumed by the French market? Have a quick look into these subjects and re-read his statement. It makes perfect sense me. I think you might be projecting American standards of living on other “western” cities without fully appreciating the differences which actually exist.

posts.name: Jens Bordasch
posts.cooked:
paul clark:

How familiar are you with French salaries? Cost of living in France? And the cultural context of spending €100 on a bottle of wine in a restaurant for french people? Are you aware of how much Latour + Burgundy is exported versus how much is consumed by the French market? Have a quick look into these subjects and re-read his statement. It makes perfect sense me. I think you might be projecting American standards of living on other “western” cities without fully appreciating the differences which actually exist.

Paul, no matter how high salaries, cost of living are in France. The director of Chateau Latour CAN definitive afford buying a € 300+ bottle in a restaurant. It’s obvious he means “I don’t want to spend that much for xyz in a restaurant”.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Well, I actually am somewhat familiar with restaurant pricing in Paris. I agree that few want to spend 300-400+E for wines w/out sufficient age and that one has at home. But, a) as others have pointed out that does not mean he can’t afford to do so; b) just as in NYC, Chicago, and other US eating cities, also in Paris, there are restaurants that do not gouge you, where the experience of the wine with fine food and service may be enjoyed.(see below). Finally, others who are ITB may know better, but in my experience (ex-roommates, friends) these are the people who easily spend the most and spend the most frequently on wine of all of those whom I know.
Here’s a sample from Les Climats, a Paris restaurant (the wine list of which I happen to have handy). I personally would be willing to pay for and happy to drink many of the '07’s with a good decant, while I and a one or a few others worked our way through a glass of champagne and a less expensive bottle of white.

1er Cru Les Corbeaux Denis Bachelet 2007 149 €
1 er Cru Les Champeaux Olivier Ber nstein 2010 267 €

posts.name: Luis Manuel Debuque Monreal Ermitaño
posts.cooked:
Carl Steefel:

What do you question about this statement? Premier Cru level or above is probably +$300/bottle. who knows what Engerer is paid? Maybe more at some places (French Laundry, Per Se, RN74). My recollection of wine prices in France is hazier–at Ma Cuisine in Beaune one might find the occasional 1er Cru at closer to 100 Euros, but…

Exactly. I make it a point to dine in Ma Cuisine at least once whenever I’m in the Côte d’Or. Though last I ate there was in April 2013, there were still several more than decent and not exceedingly young 1er crus available at around 200€ to 250€. I know Engerer has dined there at least once, because one of my ITB friends said he saw the former eating there.

Best,

N

posts.name: Hans Hauser
posts.cooked:
What a little “happy coincidence”.
Since Latour took over Eugenie I “can`t afford” it either.
It is a nice move to “buy” a Domaine and double the price of the wines because it belongs to Latour.
Guess who buys the Domaines for Mr. Pinault?
Not me.

posts.name: paul clark
posts.cooked:
A budget is a budget. Even for guys on very good salaries. Assuming he goes to restaurant “1x a month” it’s not surprising if a €300 bottle of wine doesn’t fit in with all of the other ordinary expenses. An ordinary SNCF worker in Chambolle buys a bottle of Roumier’s Musigny direct every year for €500. Anyone can afford to splurge once in a while. Affording something on a regular basis (i.e. Lunch) is a different consideration when determining if it is affordable.

His point is that the prices are too high which can seem like a bit of a double standard considering his position. But he’s selling products which are priced to the market’s demand. If anything he is saying, “you guys are willing to pay way more than these wines are worth”.

Jens Bordasch:
paul clark:

How familiar are you with
French salaries? Cost of living in France? And the cultural context of spending €100 on a bottle of wine in a restaurant for french people? Are you aware of how much Latour + Burgundy is exported versus how much is consumed by the French market? Have a quick look into these subjects and re-read his statement. It makes perfect sense me. I think you might be projecting American standards of living on other “western” cities without fully appreciating the differences which actually exist.

Paul, no matter how high salaries, cost of living are in France. The director of Chateau Latour CAN definitive afford buying a € 300+ bottle in a restaurant. It’s obvious he means “I don’t want to spend that much for xyz in a restaurant”.
